caveats: a: The available occupational composition is for NAICS 3212, not the six-digit industry.
a: Technology exposure is not the same as economically substitutable labor and includes non-AI automation.
a: The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
rho: No industry-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was found.
rho: Small LMM plants may lack clean sensor data, controls engineers, and capital for line integration.
rho: The industry's 2024 recordable injury rate of 2.5 per 100 full-time workers increases the cost of unsafe automation failures.
e: Service-like manufacturing is not separately identified in NAICS data.
e: Repeat purchases alone do not necessarily constitute an outsourced service.
e: The supplied firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
s5: Stated plans are not completed transactions.
s5: The survey spans all small-business industries and sizes.
s5: The source category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not qualify under the lens.
q: The cited public-company revenue model may not represent smaller custom or toll manufacturers.
q: Customer concentration can both accelerate pass-through and support relationship-based retention.
q: No direct study tracks five-year retention of AI-created gross benefit in this industry.
d5: Softwood-lumber elasticity is an adjacent-product proxy.
d5: Housing starts are cyclical and highly sensitive to rates and affordability.
d5: Public-company commentary is management judgment and covers markets outside the US and products outside 321219.
o: The estimate separates operator requirement from overall demand but insourcing and substitution can be difficult to distinguish.
o: Accountability requirements vary between commodity panels and engineered or tightly specified products.
o: No survey directly measures customer plans to self-supply reconstituted panels.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms earn recurring B2B revenue by manufacturing particleboard, MDF, fiberboard, OSB, or related panels to customer specifications; the broader code predominantly earns point-in-time product revenue through purchase orders.
lens.excluded_activities: Commodity panel sales with no recurring outsourced-service relationship, captive production, commodity distributors, pure software vendors, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of reconstituted wood sheets and boards that repeatedly provide custom, toll, specification-controlled, or otherwise service-like production to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts cover the broader veneer, plywood, and engineered-wood industry rather than NAICS 321219 alone; O*NET task descriptions and Census manufacturing-wide automation exposure are mapped qualitatively to the wage-weighted 321219 task mix. This likely overstates AI-only substitution because Census exposure includes robots and dedicated equipment, but may understate machine-vision and predictive-control opportunities specific to continuous panel lines.
caveats: The available occupational composition is for NAICS 3212, not the six-digit industry.
Technology exposure is not the same as economically substitutable labor and includes non-AI automation.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from 10-15 NAICS 321219 plants, including hours in inspection, monitoring, scheduling, order entry, maintenance diagnosis, and materials optimization, paired with vendor-verified AI use cases and realized labor-hour changes.
a.rationale: The ten largest occupations in the broader industry are dominated by assemblers, woodworking and sawing machine operators, material movers, truck operators, supervisors, and drivers. Their physical material-handling and machine-control work limits direct AI substitution, while inspection, production records, scheduling, quoting, maintenance prediction, process optimization, and some monitoring are exposed. Census reports 52% of manufacturing workers exposed to any automating technology, explicitly an upper bound and broader than AI; the narrower range here removes much of the physical-work exposure.

d5.bridge: Public-company commentary and Forest Service elasticities concern North American lumber, OSB, engineered panels, and softwood lumber rather than a constant-quality US NAICS 321219 service basket. Housing, renovation, and mass-timber drivers are translated into a five-year quantity ratio. This may overstate 321219 if panel substitution or import competition worsens, and understate it if OSB and MDF gain share from plywood or solid wood.
caveats: Softwood-lumber elasticity is an adjacent-product proxy.
Housing starts are cyclical and highly sensitive to rates and affordability.
Public-company commentary is management judgment and covers markets outside the US and products outside 321219.
d5.change_evidence: APA or Composite Panel Association five-year US shipment forecasts by OSB, MDF, particleboard, and hardboard, reconciled with Census housing starts, Harvard remodeling forecasts, capacity announcements, import volumes, and constant-quality end-use intensity.
d5.rationale: Residential construction is the principal demand driver: the Forest Service estimates housing-start elasticity of 0.59 for softwood lumber. West Fraser identifies construction, repair/remodeling, and industrial applications as primary uses and cites aging housing stock and mass-timber penetration as medium- and long-term supports, while warning that housing affordability and macro conditions can reduce near-term demand. A modest-growth base with a wide cyclical range is therefore appropriate.

caveats: Service-like manufacturing is not separately identified in NAICS data.
Repeat purchases alone do not necessarily constitute an outsourced service.
The supplied firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
e.change_evidence: A census of the 31 estimated LMM firms classifying revenue by commodity spot orders, repeat purchase orders, multi-year supply agreements, custom/specification manufacturing, tolling, prefinishing, and other explicit service components.
e.rationale: NAICS 321219 is defined around making sheets and boards, not outsourced services. A public producer recognizes revenue when products transfer under contracts such as purchase orders, indicating the dominant model is product sales. Only tolling, custom specification, prefinishing, or dedicated recurring production relationships plausibly meet the fixed service lens, so eligibility is a small minority of the supplied 31-firm LMM estimate.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321219 ($1,134,679,672) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321219 ($12,883,057,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $887,263 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321219); class <5: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $576,281 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $84,425 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,221,412 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $325,437 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,599,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $673,827 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,315,323 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,097,195 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,515,143 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,113,968 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $377,163,806 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,254,498 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 31

caveats: The estimate separates operator requirement from overall demand but insourcing and substitution can be difficult to distinguish.
Accountability requirements vary between commodity panels and engineered or tightly specified products.
No survey directly measures customer plans to self-supply reconstituted panels.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with building-products distributors, cabinet/furniture manufacturers, and large builders on planned insourcing, material substitution, direct imports, and willingness to buy software-defined or autonomous production without a locally accountable manufacturer.
o.rationale: The service-like activity that remains in the lens produces physical, specification-controlled panels using presses, heat, resin, material handling, and quality assurance. Software may optimize or automate work but cannot eliminate accountable plant operation. Some quantity could shift to vertically integrated customers, imports, or substitute materials, so the range is below one.

caveats: The cited public-company revenue model may not represent smaller custom or toll manufacturers.
Customer concentration can both accelerate pass-through and support relationship-based retention.
No direct study tracks five-year retention of AI-created gross benefit in this industry.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract review for eligible firms showing indexation, price-reset frequency, open-book costing, rebates, most-favored-customer clauses, bid cadence, customer concentration, and gross-margin persistence after documented productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Panel manufacturing is largely product-priced rather than hourly or cost-plus. Purchase-order repricing, customer concentration, commodity comparison, and renewal competition should transfer a substantial part of visible labor savings to buyers over five years. Process know-how, yield gains, faster lead times, and differentiated specifications can preserve some benefit, especially where changes are not transparent to customers.

caveats: No industry-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was found.
Small LMM plants may lack clean sensor data, controls engineers, and capital for line integration.
The industry's 2024 recordable injury rate of 2.5 per 100 full-time workers increases the cost of unsafe automation failures.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before/after studies from composite-panel manufacturers or equipment vendors showing deployment dates, false-reject rates, downtime, labor hours per thousand square feet, and sustained headcount avoidance for machine vision, predictive maintenance, and autonomous process control.
rho.rationale: Over five years, mature opportunities in visual inspection, predictive maintenance, scheduling, quoting, and documentation can be deployed, but integration with presses and material flows is capital-intensive and safety- and quality-sensitive. Current business AI use is still low, and physical production duties remain hard to remove. Implementation therefore captures only a minority of the exposed task opportunity in the base case.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale, gift, public offering, or other ownership-transfer plans among US small-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM reconstituted-wood manufacturers. The base uses the all-owner 14% figure; realized qualifying transfers could be lower because plans fail or involve nonqualifying gifts, while older industrial owners could make the true rate higher.
caveats: Stated plans are not completed transactions.
The survey spans all small-business industries and sizes.
The source category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not qualify under the lens.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-plan interviews for the eligible firm list plus five years of closed control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and state UCC/corporate records, distinguishing external control sales from gifts and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 14% of working business owners planned a sale, public offering, or ownership transfer within five years and 17% among owners age 55 or older. A 14% base balances likely older ownership in small industrial firms against execution failure and the exclusion of internal or non-control events.

principal_driver: Defensible gains in yield, uptime, quality inspection, and scheduling within recurring specification-driven production relationships.
principal_weakness: Most firms in the NAICS code manufacture and sell products rather than provide an outsourced service, sharply limiting lens eligibility.

caveats: The labor-opportunity primitives are inferred from a broader 3212 occupation mix, one generic O*NET occupation, and manufacturing-wide exposure to advanced automation rather than a wage-weighted six-digit task study or observed five-year AI implementation cohort.

The 9% service-lens eligibility base is an honest estimate: the sources establish that the code and public-company revenue are product-centered, but do not measure the share of LMM firms with qualifying recurring custom, toll, or dedicated-production relationships.
The transfer-rate proxy measures broad owner intentions, including gifts and rare public offerings, rather than completed external control transactions among eligible 321219 firms.

The retention estimate is not directly measured; purchase-order revenue and public-company customer concentration provide context but do not establish the five-year pass-through of AI-created benefits at small custom or toll manufacturers.
The demand range bridges from softwood-lumber elasticities and mixed-product public-company commentary, not a constant-quality five-year US 321219 service-basket forecast.

The operator-required share is a plausible physical-process estimate, but no cited evidence directly measures customer insourcing, import substitution, material substitution, or willingness to buy without an accountable local manufacturer.

sources_audited.note: Directly defines NAICS 321219 as manufacturing reconstituted wood sheets and boards and lists MDF, OSB, particleboard, and related products.

The May 2024 OEWS table for the disclosed broader veneer, plywood, and engineered-wood industry shows the cited production, machine-operation, material-movement, supervision, and driving occupation mix.
O*NET directly documents physical loading, machine operation, inspection, monitoring, and recordkeeping for production helpers, but it is one generic occupation rather than a 321219 task inventory.
Census reports 52% manufacturing-worker exposure versus 28% elsewhere and explicitly characterizes exposure as an upper bound covering advanced automating technologies.
The regulation defines reconstituted-wood presses as compacting resinated wood mats into panels through simultaneous heat and pressure, directly supporting the physical-process claim.
The 2024 BLS table reports a total recordable incidence rate of 2.5 per 100 full-time workers for NAICS 321219.

LP directly reports point-in-time product revenue under contracts such as purchase orders and 47% of 2025 sales from its top ten customers, but this large public producer does not directly represent eligible small custom or toll manufacturers.

Gallup reports the cited 14% all-owner and 17% age-55-plus five-year ownership-plan figures, but these are cross-industry intentions that include gifts and rare public offerings, not completed qualifying control transfers.
The Forest Service article reports the cited 0.59 housing-start, 0.14 income, and -0.21 price elasticities using 2000-2024 data, but for softwood lumber rather than a 321219 service basket.

West Fraser identifies construction, repair and remodeling, and industrial uses and discusses affordability, aging housing, and mass timber, but it is management outlook for a broader North American mixed-product portfolio rather than a constant-quality US 321219 forecast.
